mobile.meta.title: Lazy Patta Mobile App | Play Indian Card Games on Your Phone
mobile.meta.description: Play Lazy Patta on mobile with phone-friendly Gadha Chor, Lal Satti, private family rooms and guest play. Native app store releases are coming later.
mobile.hero.title: Play Indian family card games from your phone.
mobile.hero.body: Lazy Patta is already built for mobile browsers, so your family can open a room code, practice against computer players, or save the app to the home screen without waiting for a download.
mobile.hero.note: Native iOS and Android app store releases are planned. The mobile web app works today.
mobile.install.title: Use it like an app today
mobile.install.body: Open Lazy Patta in your phone browser and add it to your home screen for a quick app-style launch.
mobile.install.stepTwo: Choose Add to Home Screen or Install App from the browser menu.
mobile.install.stepThree: Launch Lazy Patta from the new icon when family game night starts.
mobile.games.title: Mobile-friendly games ready now
mobile.games.body: Start with a quick computer round or create a private room and share the code with family.
landing.hero.headline: Desi card games. Family game night, anywhere.
landing.hero.body: Play Gadha Chor and Lal Satti against the computer or invite your family. No betting, no complicated setup—just masti.
landing.hero.primaryCta: Start with Gadha Chor — our easiest game
landing.hero.imageAlt: A multigenerational Gujarati family playing cards together in an evening courtyard.
landing.trust.label: Why families can play safely
landing.games.title: Three family-table classics, each with its own mood.
landing.games.body: Pick the playful unmatched-card chaos of Gadha Chor, the sequence-building strategy of Lal Satti, or the trick-and-thulla tension of Jhabbu.
landing.game.familyHint: Private room, share a code. No account.
landing.game.gadhaChor.description: Make pairs, draw hidden cards, and try not to be left with the odd card while the whole table reacts.
landing.game.gadhaChor.artLabel: Gadha Chor card art: a Gulam (Jack) court card with a small donkey mascot peeking from behind a fan of face-down cards.
landing.game.lalSatti.description: Open each suit with its seven, build the sequence, and race to empty your hand first.
landing.game.lalSatti.artLabel: Lal Satti card art: the six, seven, and eight of hearts arranged as a building sequence.
landing.game.jhabbu.description: Follow the suit, watch for the Thulla, and get away before you are the last player holding cards.
landing.game.jhabbu.artLabel: Jhabbu card art: a rainy Gujarati veranda at night with a glowing lantern and a central trick pile, one off-suit card landing sideways as a Thulla.
landing.game.jhabbu.familySoon: Family rooms for Jhabbu are coming after practice mode.

landing.mode.private.title: Private Family Room

landing.mode.pass.title: Pass and Play
landing.mode.pass.body: A same-device table mode is planned for in-person family nights.
landing.family.title: Family in different cities? Still one table.
landing.family.body: A simple room code turns scattered phones into one shared game night.
landing.family.inviteTitle: Ba invited you to Gadha Chor
landing.founder.title: Why I built Lazy Patta
landing.founder.subtitle: Bringing back the family game nights we grew up with
landing.founder.p1: I remember summer vacations when the whole family would gather—whether we were travelling, visiting relatives, or simply spending an evening at home.
landing.founder.p2: Nobody cared about the television. Phones were forgotten. A deck of cards was enough to fill the room with laughter, small rivalries, teasing, and love.
landing.founder.p3: We played the desi patta games passed down through our families. Of course, everyone wanted to win—but the best part was always being together.
landing.founder.p4: Lazy Patta is my attempt to bring a little of that feeling back.
landing.founder.p5: Whether your family is sitting around the same table or joining from different cities and countries, I hope Lazy Patta helps you pause, play, laugh, and create memories together again.
landing.founder.credit: Built by Ishani Bhatt, creator of Lazy Traveler.
landing.founder.photoAlt: Ishani Bhatt, creator of Lazy Patta
landing.coming.title: More Indian family games are warming up.
landing.footer.label: Footer navigation
landing.footer.descriptor: The Indian card games we grew up playing.

jhabbu.quickRuleGoal: Empty your hand and get away before one player is left holding cards.

games.gadhaChor.metaDescription: Play Gadha Chor online with family. Also known as Gulaam Chor, Gulam Chor, Gaddha Chor or Jack Thief, this no-betting card game is easy to start.

games.gadhaChor.otherNamesSummary: Some families search for this game as Gulaam Chor, Gulam Chor, Gaddha Chor or Jack Thief. It is similar to Old Maid because players clear pairs and avoid the final unmatched card, but household rules can differ.
games.gadhaChor.howBody: Players clear matching pairs, then take one hidden card on each turn. If the drawn card makes a pair, the pair leaves the hand. The player left with the unmatched card becomes Gadha Chor.
games.gadhaChor.oldMaidTitle: Is Gadha Chor the same as Old Maid?
games.gadhaChor.oldMaidBody: Gadha Chor is close to Old Maid in spirit: make pairs and avoid the odd card. Lazy Patta keeps the Indian family-table naming and rules instead of presenting it as the exact same game.

rules.gadhaChor.metaTitle: How to Play Gadha Chor — Gulam Chor Rules | Lazy Patta
rules.gadhaChor.metaDescription: Learn how to play Gadha Chor, also known as Gulam Chor or Jack Thief. Simple rules, setup, scoring, family variations and a free practice game.
rules.gadhaChor.heading: How to Play Gadha Chor
rules.gadhaChor.intro: Gadha Chor is a light, fast family card game where players clear matching pairs and try not to be left holding the one unmatched card. It is widely known as Gulam Chor, Gulaam Chor, Gaddha Chor or Jack Thief, and it plays much like the game English speakers call Old Maid.
rules.gadhaChor.players: Best with 3 to 6 players. A round takes about 5 to 10 minutes, so it suits quick turns between chai and dinner. The rules are simple enough for children and grandparents to play at the same table.
rules.gadhaChor.setup: Use a standard 52-card deck. For the classic donkey feel, one card is left without a partner — traditionally three of the four Jacks (Gulams) stay in, leaving one lonely Jack that becomes the card nobody wants. Deal all the cards out; some players may get one more than others, and that is fine.
rules.gadhaChor.objective: Get rid of every card in your hand by making pairs. You do not want to be the last player still holding a card when everyone else is empty — that player becomes the Gadha Chor for the round.
rules.gadhaChor.play: First, everyone removes any pairs already in their hand and sets them aside. Then, on each turn, a player fans their cards face-down and the player to their left draws one at random. If the drawn card completes a pair, both cards are discarded; if not, it stays in the hand. Play continues around the table until only one unmatched card is left.
rules.gadhaChor.end: The round ends when every player but one has emptied their hand. The player still holding the odd card is the Gadha Chor. The classic game has no points — families simply note who became the donkey and deal again. If you prefer a score, give the Gadha Chor one point each round and the lowest total after several rounds wins.
rules.gadhaChor.variations: Some families remove a different card instead of a Jack, or keep the full deck and let two odd cards float. Others add a gentle forfeit for the Gadha Chor, like fetching water or telling a joke. Lazy Patta keeps the friendly Indian table naming rather than presenting it as the exact Old Maid ruleset.
rules.gadhaChor.mistakes: The most common mistake is forgetting to discard pairs you already hold at the start of the round. Another is holding your fan so the unmatched card is easy to guess — keep your face-down cards well shuffled so no one can read them.
rules.gadhaChor.strategy: Shuffle your hand before offering it, so the drawer cannot tell where the unmatched card sits. When you draw, watch which cards others have taken to guess who might be holding the lonely Jack, and try not to be the player passing it on last.
rules.gadhaChor.faq.q1: Is Gadha Chor the same as Old Maid?
rules.gadhaChor.faq.a1: Very close. Both are pair-matching games where you avoid the final unmatched card. Gadha Chor keeps the Indian family-table names and small rule differences instead of being presented as the exact same game.
rules.gadhaChor.faq.q2: How many people can play Gadha Chor?
rules.gadhaChor.faq.a2: Three to six players works best. With more people you can combine two decks, though hands become shorter and rounds go faster.
rules.gadhaChor.faq.q3: Do I need real cards to play?
rules.gadhaChor.faq.a3: No. You can practise Gadha Chor for free against the computer on Lazy Patta, or start a private room and play with family from separate phones.
rules.lalSatti.metaTitle: How to Play Lal Satti — Badam Saat Rules | Lazy Patta
rules.lalSatti.metaDescription: Learn Lal Satti rules, setup and scoring. Also known as Badam Saat, Sevens or Seven of Hearts — start with 7♥, build every suit and empty your hand first.
rules.lalSatti.heading: How to Play Lal Satti
rules.lalSatti.intro: Lal Satti is a sequence-building card game where players lay cards in order on the table, suit by suit, starting from the sevens. It is widely known as Badam Saat, Badam Satti, Laal Satti, Sevens or Seven of Hearts, and it rewards patience over luck.
rules.lalSatti.players: Best with 3 to 8 players. A round usually takes 10 to 15 minutes. There is very little to memorise, so it is an easy game to teach at a family gathering.
rules.lalSatti.setup: Use a standard 52-card deck and deal all the cards out evenly between the players. There is no separate draw pile — every card is in someone's hand from the start, and the table layout grows as sevens are played.
rules.lalSatti.objective: Be the first player to empty your hand by adding cards to the sequences on the table. Cards you cannot yet play stay with you, so opening suits early keeps your options open.
rules.lalSatti.play: The player holding the seven of hearts (7♥) starts by placing it in the middle. Each suit opens only when its seven is played, after which cards build downward toward the ace and upward toward the king. On your turn you play one legal card that extends a suit, or lay a suit's seven to open it. If you have no legal move, you pass.
rules.lalSatti.end: The round ends the moment a player lays down their last card — that player wins. For scoring, count the cards left in every other player's hand: each remaining card is one point, and points are bad. Play several rounds and the lowest total wins the game.
rules.lalSatti.variations: Some families require you to play a card whenever you legally can, so you cannot hold a seven back to block others; stricter tables allow blocking as a tactic. Others start from a fixed suit or deal a few cards face down. Lal Satti keeps the classic 7♥ opening as the standard.
rules.lalSatti.mistakes: The biggest mistake is sitting on your sevens too long — until a suit's seven is down, nobody can play that suit, and you may get stuck holding high and low cards you cannot place. Passing when you actually have a legal move is another common slip.
rules.lalSatti.strategy: Play your sevens early to open suits and free up your other cards. Watch which suits are blocked and keep the cards that let your own runs flow. Late in the round, count what is left in hand so you know whether to open a suit or protect your lead.
rules.lalSatti.faq.q1: Why does Lal Satti start with the seven of hearts?
rules.lalSatti.faq.a1: The seven of hearts opens the table and gives the game its common English name, Seven of Hearts. From there each suit grows into a visible sequence once its own seven is played.
rules.lalSatti.faq.q2: What are the other names for Lal Satti?
rules.lalSatti.faq.a2: Families call it Badam Saat, Badam Satti, Laal Satti, Sevens, Satte Pe Satta or Seven of Hearts. Lazy Patta uses Lal Satti as the primary name and keeps the others for discovery.
rules.lalSatti.faq.q3: How do you win Lal Satti?
rules.lalSatti.faq.a3: Empty your hand before everyone else by adding cards to the table sequences. If you play for points, the player with the fewest cards left across several rounds wins.
lalSattiTutorial.sevensBody: Each suit opens with its seven, laid down first to start that suit's row.
lalSattiTutorial.sequenceBody: On your turn, play a card that extends a suit up or down from its seven — or pass if you can't.
lalSattiTutorial.winBody: First to play every card wins the round.
settings.largeCardsHint: Larger ranks and a focused-card view while keeping your full hand available.

games.jhabbu.metaTitle: Play Jhabbu Online | Bhabho & Get Away Card Game | Lazy Patta
games.jhabbu.metaDescription: Jhabbu is a fast Indian shedding card game, also known as Bhabho, Bhabhi, Laad and Get Away. Follow the led suit, survive the Thulla, and empty your hand first. Coming soon to Lazy Patta.
games.jhabbu.detailIntro: Jhabbu is a fast Indian shedding card game, also known as Bhabho, Bhabhi, Laad and Get Away. Follow the led suit, avoid getting stuck with the pile, and empty your hand before everyone else. Computer play is coming soon — learn the rules now.
games.jhabbu.otherNamesSummary: Many families search for this game as Bhabho, Bhabhi, Laad, Get Away or Zabbu. It is a follow-the-suit shedding game, but household rules for the Thulla and who picks up the pile can differ.
games.jhabbu.howBody: The player with the Ace of Spades leads. Everyone must follow the led suit if they can. When all cards follow, the pile clears; but if someone cannot follow and throws an off-suit Thulla, the highest led-suit card picks up the whole pile. Empty your hand to get away.
games.jhabbu.thullaBody: A Thulla is an off-suit card played when you cannot follow the led suit. Instead of clearing, the player who laid the highest card of the originally led suit must pick up the entire pile. The Thulla is how the pile suddenly lands on someone.
games.jhabbu.getawayBody: The moment your hand is empty you have got away and are safe for the round. Play continues until only one player is left holding cards — that player is the Bhabhi. Getting away early is the whole goal.
games.jhabbu.familyBody: Jhabbu is coming to Lazy Patta as a private-room and computer game. For now you can learn the rules and family variations; Play buttons will appear the moment computer mode is ready.
rules.jhabbu.metaTitle: How to Play Jhabbu — Bhabho, Bhabhi & Get Away Rules | Lazy Patta
rules.jhabbu.metaDescription: Learn how to play Jhabbu, also known as Bhabho, Bhabhi, Laad or Get Away. The Ace of Spades opening, following suit, the Thulla, who picks up the pile, and how to get away.
rules.jhabbu.heading: How to Play Jhabbu
rules.jhabbu.intro: Jhabbu is a fast, social Indian card game where you follow the led suit and try to empty your hand before everyone else. It is widely known as Bhabho, Bhabhi, Laad and Get Away. Watch out for the Thulla — the off-suit card that drops the whole pile on one player.
rules.jhabbu.players: Best with 3 to 6 players and a single standard 52-card deck. A game runs about 15 to 30 minutes. It is easy to pick up in one round and rewards watching what everyone else discards.
rules.jhabbu.setup: Deal the whole deck out as evenly as possible; some players may hold one extra card. There are no teams. The player holding the Ace of Spades opens the very first trick.
rules.jhabbu.objective: Empty your hand as fast as you can. Players who run out of cards get away and are safe. The one player still holding cards when everyone else is empty loses the round and is the Bhabhi.
rules.jhabbu.play: The lead player lays a card and everyone in turn must follow the led suit if they hold it. If every card follows suit, the highest card wins the trick and leads next. The pile is only picked up when a Thulla is thrown — otherwise the cards are cleared and play continues.
rules.jhabbu.end: The round ends when only one player still holds cards. That player is the Bhabhi for the round. Classic play keeps no score — families just note who was left holding the cards. If you prefer, give the last player one point per round and the lowest total wins.
rules.jhabbu.variations: Households differ on the Thulla: some let you throw an off-suit card whenever you cannot follow, others restrict it. Some require the Ace of Spades to open every hand, others only the first. Lazy Patta keeps the friendly Indian names — Bhabho, Bhabhi, Laad, Get Away — over a single official ruleset.
rules.jhabbu.mistakes: The most common mistake is wasting high cards early and getting stuck holding them at the end. Another is forgetting that a Thulla sends the pile to the highest card of the led suit, not to the player who threw the off-suit card.
rules.jhabbu.strategy: Shed high cards when it is safe, and keep a low card in a common suit so you can follow without winning the trick. Watch which suits opponents cannot follow — that is where a well-timed Thulla hands them the whole pile.
rules.jhabbu.faq.q1: Why is Jhabbu also called Bhabhi or Get Away?
rules.jhabbu.faq.a1: It is the same follow-the-suit shedding game under several regional names — Bhabho, Bhabhi, Laad and Get Away. 'Get away' is what you do when you empty your hand; 'Bhabhi' is a playful name for whoever is left holding cards at the end.
rules.jhabbu.faq.q2: What is a Thulla in Jhabbu?
rules.jhabbu.faq.a2: A Thulla is an off-suit card played when you cannot follow the led suit. It forces the player who laid the highest card of the originally led suit to pick up the entire pile, changing who gets stuck.
rules.jhabbu.faq.q3: Can I play Jhabbu on Lazy Patta yet?
rules.jhabbu.faq.a3: Not yet — Jhabbu is coming soon. You can learn the full rules now, and we will add computer practice and private family rooms as soon as the game mode is ready.
